project_title: Run a Hiring Loop for One Role

steps.q: Step 1: The engineering director needs a new Data Scientist. What is the very first step of the Hiring Loop?

options.text: Conduct an Intake Meeting to define the must-have skills, salary bounds, and interview panel clearly.
Start messaging random people on LinkedIn.
Post the job title to a job board with no description.
Call an agency and tell them to find someone fast.

steps.q: Step 2: You review the draft Job Description (JD). It lists 25 'Required Skills'. What is your strategic advice?

options.text: Advise the manager to separate 'Must-Haves' (core competencies) from 'Nice-to-Haves' to avoid filtering out diverse, capable talent.
Add 10 more skills just in case.
Delete the skills section entirely.
Keep it; we only want unicorns.

steps.q: Step 3: To ensure an unbiased evaluation process during the loop, you must mandate the use of:

options.text: A standardized interview scorecard anchored to specific behavioral traits and technical competencies.
A 'vibe check' by the team.
A subjective rating out of 10 based on gut feeling.
Reviewing their social media profiles together.

steps.q: Step 4: You begin sourcing. The pipeline is heavily skewed toward one demographic. What is the immediate correction?

options.text: Expand sourcing channels to diverse professional networks and audit the JD language for exclusionary terminology.
Lower the technical bar entirely.
Ignore it, talent is talent.
Only interview that demographic to save time.

steps.q: Step 5: During the HR phone screen, what are the three critical data points you must capture?

options.text: Salary expectations, notice period/availability, and primary motivation for role alignment.
Their favorite movie, their age, and their marital status.
Their ability to code, their IQ, and their typing speed.
Nothing, just talk about the weather.

steps.q: Step 6: The candidate progresses to the Technical Interview. How should the technical panel be prepped?

options.text: Assign specific technical domains to specific interviewers to avoid asking the candidate the same coding question three times.
Tell everyone to ask whatever they want.
Give them no prep; they are senior engineers.
Tell them to try and make the candidate cry.

steps.q: Step 7: The candidate finishes all rounds. It's time for the Debrief. What is the rule of the debrief meeting?

options.text: All interviewers must submit their scorecards BEFORE the meeting to prevent 'Anchoring Bias' from the most senior person.
The most senior person speaks first and tells everyone what to think.
You calculate an average score and don't discuss it.
You let the CEO decide blindly.

steps.q: Step 8: The panel says 'Hire'. You run references. One reference says the candidate is 'brilliant but hard to work with'. You:

options.text: Probe the reference for specific behavioral examples and contextualize this data with the interview panel's behavioral read.
Immediately reject the candidate.
Ignore the reference entirely.
Tell the candidate what their reference said.

steps.q: Step 9: You move to Offer Negotiation. The candidate asks for 15% above the approved band. Your tactic is:

options.text: Highlight the total rewards package, understand what drives them (e.g., equity, flexibility), and consult finance for a counter-strategy.
Hang up the phone.
Immediately say yes without approval.
Yell at them for being greedy.

steps.q: Step 10: The candidate accepts the offer! To cleanly close the Hiring Loop, what is the final administrative action?

options.text: Send professional, constructive rejection updates to other finalists, run background checks, and transition the hire to Onboarding.
Ghost the other candidates.
Go to sleep.
Keep interviewing just in case they back out.
